questions.text: I hold the view that the moral priority of suffering is a truth that becomes more obvious, not less, with experience.
I often replay emotional moments in my mind and re-experience them at nearly full intensity.
I am drawn to roles where I can shape outcomes directly.
I find that planning reduces stress because I know what to expect.
I feel a strong pull to finish what I start, even when enthusiasm fades.
I often start projects without a clear plan, trusting that the path will reveal itself.
I tend not to register when someone's reply carries a hint of sarcasm.
I hold the view that the moral demand of suffering is clearest when I understand not just the fact of it but the full human context surrounding it.
I make choices based on present circumstances and adapt to downstream effects as they appear.
I believe that if I have a roof over my head and someone else does not, something is mine to give.
I tend to pick vacations based on what I want to see and do, not on what others prefer.
I believe that a calm, analytical approach to human pain is not coldness but a higher form of engagement with the problem.
I experience reunion with people I care about as warm and welcome without it being an emotional avalanche.
I experience worry as a passing thought rather than an escalating physical state.
I would rather be the founder than the early employee.
I believe that the hours between 6 a.m. and 9 a.m. should belong entirely to my personal development.
I believe that the conversations I have should leave the other person feeling valued, even if it costs me time.
I feel that celebrating someone else's achievement is more satisfying than celebrating my own.
I believe investing in my own development is one of the most important things I can do.
I find it easy to go along with the group's direction, even when I have reservations.
I believe that social environments are rich with information most people overlook.
I experience the passage of emotional time at a steady pace — no moments feel stretched or compressed by feeling.
I do not naturally pick up on the difference between someone sharing a vulnerability and someone testing how I will respond to it.
I feel that I absorb the emotional tone of a room the moment I walk in.
I believe pivot-or-persist decisions should be made by the group together.
I prefer to build agreement organically rather than persuade.
I believe a project needs one clear leader to succeed.
I believe that the felt weight of suffering reveals moral truths that logical analysis alone would miss.
I tend not to notice when two people share a meaningful look during a conversation.
I hold the view that remaining objective during emotionally intense experiences is a capacity that improves with practice and is worth cultivating.
Even a glancing disappointment registers in me as a significant blow.
I notice when someone slightly raises their eyebrows in a micro-reaction before composing their face.
I am meticulous about keeping my appointments and rarely cancel or reschedule.
I believe that processing emotionally charged situations through a framework of analysis rather than feeling is a mature and effective approach.
I find that formalizing my goals can make them feel like obligations rather than aspirations.
I experience silence after an argument as almost as intense as the argument itself.
